preFix.title: Building a garden trellis panel
preFix.subtitle: Construct a square-pattern timber trellis from sawn batten
preFix.excerpt: How to build a simple square-grid garden trellis panel from pressure-treated or painted sawn timber batten, including setting out the grid, lap-jointing or simply nailing the crossings, and mounting the panel on wooden spacer blocks away from the wall.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. RHS gardening guidance on climbing plant support. Timber Research and Development Association timber treatment notes.

content.text: A simple square-grid trellis panel is a beginner woodworking project, but longevity depends on material choice and correct attachment to the wall.
is essential for outdoor use: untreated softwood rots in two to three years in UK conditions. The panel must be held away from the wall on
: a panel pressed flat against the wall traps moisture and stains the wall face.
Step 1, calculate and cut the batten

Standard trellis uses 38 × 19 mm pressure-treated sawn batten for the frame and 25 × 19 mm for the infill strips, with a grid spacing of 150 mm. Calculate the number of vertical and horizontal strips needed, adding 10% for waste. Cut all strips to length using the panel saw against a square cut-line marked with the combination square and pencil. Stack the cut pieces in bundles by length.

Assemble the outer perimeter frame on a flat surface. Butt-join the corners with 65 mm galvanised nails driven from two directions, or pre-drill and fix with galvanised screws. Check the frame is square by measuring both diagonals: they must be equal. If not, rack the frame gently until the diagonals match, then fix a temporary diagonal brace across one corner until the infill strips lock the shape.

Lay the horizontal strips first, spacing them evenly at 150 mm centres and nailing through the frame into each end with a single galvanised nail. Lay the vertical strips over the top, nailing at each frame crossing. At each intersection of horizontal and vertical strip, drive a single galvanised nail down through the vertical into the horizontal: this locks the grid. For a stronger
at each crossing, cut half the depth of each strip at all intersection positions before assembly.
Step 4, fix spacer blocks to the wall

Fix 50 × 50 × 50 mm pressure-treated timber spacer blocks to the wall at the four corners and at the mid-point of each long side. Use appropriate fixings for the wall type: No. 10 × 60 mm screws with wall plugs for masonry, or coach screws into fence post anchor plates for freestanding fixing. Check the blocks are at the correct height with the spirit level. The front face of each block should be in the same plane.
Step 5, fix the panel to the spacers

Lift the trellis panel into position and drive 65 mm galvanised screws through the frame into each spacer block. If the panel needs to be lowered for painting the wall behind, do not fix the top permanently. Use hook-and-eye fixings at the top instead, so the panel can be unhooked and leant away from the wall.

A diagonal grid (diamond pattern) is made by rotating the grid 45° within the frame: the strips run at 45° to the horizontal. The cut lengths vary and the corners require mitred or coped cuts, making it a slightly harder project. Ready-made expandable trellis panels (the scissor-action type) are a quick alternative but are structurally weaker and do not take heavy climbers such as wisteria or climbing roses.

attrs.fix: While the nails are still slightly mobile, rack the frame until both diagonals are equal, then add the infill strips to lock the square. A fixed frame may need one corner joint removed and resquared.
attrs.cause: Frame assembled without checking diagonal measurements; work surface not flat.
attrs.symptom: Trellis panel is not square after assembly

attrs.fix: Relocate fixings into solid masonry or structural studs. Pack behind any spacer block that sits slightly low to bring all blocks into the same plane.
attrs.cause: Spacer blocks fixed to plasterboard rather than masonry or stud; spacer blocks not co-planar.
attrs.symptom: Panel moves or rocks on the wall after fixing

attrs.fix: No repair — replace with correctly pressure-treated UC2 or UC3 timber. Ensure spacers are in place before refitting.
attrs.cause: Untreated or minimally treated timber used; panel in direct contact with the wall trapping moisture.
attrs.symptom: Trellis starts to rot within two seasons

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
brand-trademark: brand name "Anchor" — generic equivalent is "salted butter"
